lessons.subject: Science
lessons.title: Science and Technology
lessons.objectives:
Understanding of basic of Science and Technology
Provide overview of Science advancement in real world
Provide different understanding on application of Science and Technology
lessons.difficulty: Difficulty in Seeing | Difficulty in Remembering / Concentrating
lessons.indicators: Struggles to complete tasks within the allotted time | Frequently requests help even for familiar tasks
lessons.generated_output:
DAILY LESSON PLAN

School: ___________________________     Grade Level: ALS     Quarter: Q1
Teacher: _____________________________________________________
Learning Area: Science
Teaching Date and Time: ________________________

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

I. CURRICULUM CONTENT, STANDARDS AND LESSON COMPETENCIES

A. Content Standards
The learner demonstrates understanding of the foundational concepts, historical progression, and practical applications of science and technology in everyday life.

B. Performance Standards
The learner independently identifies and explains how scientific advancements and technological tools are applied to address real-world needs and improve daily living.

C. Learning Competencies and Objectives
Define the relationship between science and technology; describe key scientific advancements and their real-world impact; demonstrate understanding of how technology is applied across different sectors such as health, communication, and agriculture.

D. Content
Topic: Science and Technology
Introduction to Science and Technology: Core definitions, historical milestones in scientific discovery, and practical applications of modern technology in community and household contexts.

E. Integration
Inclusive Education Focus: Difficulty in Seeing | Difficulty in Remembering / Concentrating
Integrates functional literacy through simplified reading and vocabulary building, numeracy through basic data interpretation on technology usage, and life skills through problem-solving scenarios that connect scientific principles to daily routines.

F. Support Plan (Accommodations and Modifications)
Accommodations:
1. Provide all instructional materials in large print (minimum 18pt), high-contrast formatting, and uncluttered layouts to reduce visual strain and improve readability. 2. Implement structured chunking with 2-minute sensory or movement breaks every 10 minutes to sustain attention and mitigate cognitive fatigue. 3. Utilize a visual step-by-step task checklist paired with a digital timer to support self-pacing and time management. 4. Permit verbal responses, audio recordings, or pointing to answer choices instead of written output to reduce working memory load and support expression.

Modifications:
1. Reduce task volume by limiting matching and sorting activities to 4-6 high-yield items that directly align with core lesson objectives. 2. Replace dense reading passages with simplified, bulleted summaries accompanied by labeled diagrams and real-object demonstrations. 3. Convert abstract text-based assessments into hands-on, concrete sorting tasks using physical objects or tactile picture cards. 4. Extend all time limits by 50% and allow task completion across two shorter instructional blocks, prioritizing conceptual mastery over speed.

Observed Manifestations & Additional Notes:
Supports align with documented strategies for concentration and memory difficulties, emphasizing structured pacing, visual chunking, and reduced cognitive load to mitigate attention fatigue and improve task completion. No additional individualized supports were specified; teacher will monitor for emerging needs and adjust scaffolding accordingly.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

II. LEARNING RESOURCES
Large-print visual guides with high-contrast formatting, tactile models of everyday tools, audio-recorded lesson summaries, simplified reference cards with labeled diagrams, real-world technology samples (e.g., flashlight, water filter, mobile device), and a visual pacing timer.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

III. TEACHING AND LEARNING PROCEDURE

A. Activating Prior Knowledge
Duration: 5 minutes
Basic familiarity with common household tools and digital devices; awareness that scientific inquiry explains natural phenomena and technology creates practical solutions to human needs.

B. Establishing Lesson Purpose
Duration: 5 minutes
To establish foundational knowledge of science and technology while implementing targeted visual, memory, and attention supports, enabling the learner to confidently recognize, retain, and apply scientific concepts to real-world contexts.

C. Developing and Deepening Understanding
Duration: 25 minutes
Teacher's Activity:
Introduction: Begin with high-contrast, large-print visuals of familiar technology paired with brief audio narration to activate prior knowledge and establish relevance. Guided Practice: Teacher models matching scientific concepts to real-world tools using chunked, step-by-step demonstrations. Use a visual timer to segment work into 5-minute intervals, providing verbal check-ins and think-aloud strategies to sustain attention. Independent Practice: Learner completes a simplified 4-item sorting task using picture cards and a reference guide. Extended time is provided, and the learner uses a self-monitoring checklist to track progress. Feedback: Teacher delivers immediate, specific verbal praise and corrective guidance using a 'two strengths and one next step' framework. Class reviews one common misconception with a concrete, hands-on demonstration to reinforce accurate understanding.

D. Making Generalization
Duration: 5 minutes
Learners will apply the lesson by identifying one scientific principle behind a household item at home and explaining its practical benefit to a family member or peer, reinforcing transfer of knowledge beyond the classroom environment.

E. Evaluating Learning
Duration: 10 minutes
Adaptive Assessment Methods:
Formative assessment through direct observation of task completion, accuracy in the hands-on sorting activity, and verbal explanation of one real-world application. A simplified 3-item exit ticket with large print, visual cues, and optional verbal response will assess retention of core concepts.

F. Teacher's Remarks
Learner engaged effectively with tactile and large-print materials but required additional prompting during the independent sorting phase. Visual timer and chunked breaks significantly improved on-task behavior and reduced help-seeking frequency. Next session will incorporate peer modeling and gradual prompt fading to build independent task initiation.

G. Reflection
The integration of multi-sensory resources and structured pacing effectively addressed concentration and memory challenges, consistent with clinical guidance on supporting sustained attention and working memory. I will continue to monitor the balance between scaffolding and independence, gradually reducing visual checklists as retention improves. Future lessons will include more community-based problem scenarios to maintain relevance and engagement.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PREPARED BY: ___________________________

REVIEWED BY: ___________________________

NOTED BY: ___________________________

lessons.title: Basics of Mathematics
lessons.difficulty: Difficulty in Basic Learning and Applying Knowledge
lessons.indicators: Struggles to complete tasks within the allotted time | Needs prompts to stay focused on classroom activities

lessons.generated_output:
DAILY LESSON PLAN

School: ___________________________     Grade Level: ALS     Quarter: Q1
Teacher: _____________________________________________________
Learning Area: Mathematics
Teaching Date and Time: ________________________

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

I. CURRICULUM CONTENT, STANDARDS AND LESSON COMPETENCIES

A. Content Standards
Functional Academics in Mathematics aims to strengthen knowledge, skills, attitudes, and values to prepare learners for self-reliance and productive citizenship, as outlined in the K to 12 SPED Transition Curriculum.

B. Performance Standards
Learners demonstrate basic mathematical skills necessary for daily living and functional independence, applying foundational concepts with appropriate support and accommodations.

C. Learning Competencies and Objectives
Recognize and sequence basic numbers, identify simple math symbols (+, -), and perform foundational counting and calculation tasks using multi-sensory and visual supports.

D. Content
Topic: Basics of Mathematics
Basics of Mathematics: Number recognition (1-10), counting sequences, and introduction to basic operation symbols.

E. Integration
Inclusive Education Focus: Difficulty in Basic Learning and Applying Knowledge
Integrates functional academics with daily living skills, emphasizing practical application of numeracy to foster self-reliance and social interaction as guided by the transition curriculum.

F. Observed Manifestations & Accommodations
N/A

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

II. LEARNING RESOURCES
Large-print number flashcards, tactile counting manipulatives (blocks/counters), visual schedule/timer, adapted worksheets with clear spacing, prompt cue cards, and oral response recording sheets.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

III. TEACHING AND LEARNING PROCEDURE

A. Activating Prior Knowledge
Duration: 5 minutes
Basic familiarity with everyday quantities, routine counting, and recognition of common objects used in daily life.

B. Establishing Lesson Purpose
Duration: 5 minutes
To build foundational numeracy skills using structured, multi-sensory instruction that accommodates reading/writing difficulties and attention challenges, while promoting functional independence and task completion.

C. Developing and Deepening Understanding
Duration: 25 minutes
Teacher's Activity:
1. Introduction: Present numbers 1-10 using large visual cards paired with physical objects. Provide short, clear oral instructions with visual cues to reduce cognitive load. 2. Guided Practice: Chunk activities into 5-minute segments. Use manipulatives for hands-on counting. Provide frequent, positive verbal and visual prompts to maintain focus. Allow extended time for processing. 3. Independent Practice: Learners match numbers to quantities using adapted, low-writing worksheets. Use a visual timer to structure work periods. Offer immediate, constructive feedback. 4. Support Application: Implement accommodations (extended time, reduced writing demands, oral responses) and adaptive assessment methods (checklist-based observation, matching tasks) throughout.

D. Making Generalization
Duration: 5 minutes
Connect number recognition and counting to real-life routines (e.g., counting items, simple budgeting, organizing materials). Encourage learners to apply skills independently in daily contexts to reinforce self-reliance and functional academics goals.

E. Evaluating Learning
Duration: 10 minutes
Adaptive Assessment Methods:
Adaptive assessment using observation checklists, oral questioning, and hands-on matching tasks. Focus on accuracy, engagement, and task completion with support rather than speed or traditional written output. Document progress using performance rubrics aligned with functional math competencies.

F. Teacher's Remarks
Learner required frequent verbal and visual prompts to sustain attention. Extended time was necessary for task completion. Tactile manipulatives and chunked instructions significantly improved engagement and reduced frustration related to writing/reading demands.

G. Reflection
The structured, multi-sensory approach effectively addressed attention and processing challenges. Future lessons will incorporate more movement-based counting activities and explicit sequencing practice to further support dyslexia/dysgraphia indicators and improve sustained focus.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

REVIEWED BY: ___________________________

NOTED BY: ___________________________

lessons.generated_output:
DAILY LESSON PLAN

School: ___________________________     Grade Level: ALS     Quarter: Q1
Teacher: _____________________________________________________
Learning Area: English
Teaching Date and Time: ________________________

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

I. CURRICULUM CONTENT, STANDARDS AND LESSON COMPETENCIES

A. Content Standards
English: Learners are expected to improve and effectively use their communicative, as well as social, skills through learning opportunities in oral and written communication. Math: Learners are expected to recognize and organize numbers according to value up to 20 and demonstrate understanding of the concept of whole numbers.

B. Performance Standards
Demonstrate understanding of whole numbers; recognize and organize numbers up to 20; count objects with one-to-one correspondence up to quantities of 20; improve oral and written communication skills.

C. Learning Competencies and Objectives
Oral Language, Vocabulary, Writing, Number Values, Counting, One-to-One Correspondence

D. Content
Topic: Basics of Mathematics
1. Introduction to numbers 1-10 using large print and tactile cards.
2. Counting objects with one-to-one correspondence up to 20 using manipulatives.
3. Simple oral counting drills integrated with English vocabulary (e.g., "one apple", "two bananas").
4. Guided writing activity: tracing numbers and writing simple number words with support.
5. Group activity: matching number cards to written words.

E. Integration
Inclusive Education Focus: Difficulty in Communication | Difficulty in Mobility | Difficulty in Basic Learning and Applying Knowledge
English and Math concepts are blended by using number words in oral and written language activities, reinforcing both counting and vocabulary skills.

F. Observed Manifestations & Accommodations
Use large print and tactile numbers; allow extra time for writing; provide a writing guide with number shapes; use a speech‑to‑text tool for written responses if needed.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

II. LEARNING RESOURCES
Large print number cards, tactile number blocks, manipulatives (buttons, beads), simple worksheets with high-contrast print, assistive writing tools (large grip pens), audio recordings of number words, whiteboard with large markers.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

III. TEACHING AND LEARNING PROCEDURE

A. Activating Prior Knowledge
Duration: 5 minutes
Basic familiarity with numbers 1-5, ability to identify simple shapes, basic oral language skills.

B. Establishing Lesson Purpose
Duration: 5 minutes
To introduce and reinforce basic mathematical concepts of counting and number recognition while simultaneously developing oral and written English communication skills.

C. Developing and Deepening Understanding
Duration: 25 minutes
Teacher's Activity:
Number sense, oral language fluency, writing accuracy, fine motor skills for writing numbers, self‑reliance in completing tasks.

D. Making Generalization
Duration: 5 minutes
Students will apply counting skills in everyday contexts (e.g., counting items in a grocery list) and use number words in simple sentences.

E. Evaluating Learning
Duration: 10 minutes
Adaptive Assessment Methods:
Observation of counting accuracy, oral questioning about number words, completion of a simple matching worksheet, teacher check‑list of writing attempts.

F. Teacher's Remarks
Provide seating close to teacher to reduce mobility strain; use a small table to accommodate assistive devices.

G. Reflection
Consider whether the tactile materials helped maintain engagement; assess if the oral‑written integration supported students with dysgraphia.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PREPARED BY: ___________________________

REVIEWED BY: ___________________________

NOTED BY: ___________________________

reflections.subject: Example - Mathematics
reflections.lesson_plan_linked: Example - Comparing Fractions with Visual Models
reflections.strategies_used: Used concrete fraction strips and think-pair-share.
reflections.learner_response: Most learners engaged better after visual scaffolds were introduced.
reflections.worked_well: Guided examples and peer support increased participation.
reflections.needs_improvement: Need clearer pacing for independent practice.
reflections.challenges: A few learners needed repeated instructions.
reflections.next_steps: Prepare shorter task chunks and add recap prompts.

reflections.subject: Example - Science
reflections.strategies_used: Used station rotation and visual anchor charts.
reflections.learner_response: Learners collaborated well in small groups.
reflections.next_steps: Add timed cues and assign transition roles.

observations.subject: Example - Mathematics, Grade 5
observations.notes: Learners benefited from visual cues and flexible grouping.

observations.subject: Example - Science, Grade 6
observations.notes: Clear routines and transitions improved class flow.

pre: I can plan lessons that accommodate learners with diverse needs.
I can modify lesson objectives appropriately for learners with SEN.
I feel confident selecting inclusive support strategies.
I can contextualize lessons to meet the needs of my diverse learners.
I know how to document reflections after teaching an inclusive lesson.
